speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number eight. What happened after you left Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I left Dachau, oh, I flew back to Frankfurt. I couldn't stand the smell of myself or my uniform and I burned it. I burned everything-- my clothes, my pants, my shoes, my socks, my flight jacket, which I had to pay for, by the way. It belonged to Uncle Sam. And I couldn't get rid of the smell. Went to see the flight surgeon, who told me he knew what was going on, and it eventually would go away, and it did. It-- if I think about it right now, I can still-- not like it was then. But I could smell it on my hand, and the airplane, I wiped off all the control panels that I touched, thinking that it's all over. And that's the way it was. It was really a paranoia. Sick. Didn't surprise the shrink, though. He knew. [LAUGHING] I mean, you know. Bergen-Belsen, as I said, went back the second time. The period of four, five, six days. I think it was six days. And 26,000 people had died and they were burying them by then in the pits with bulldozers. Whereas before, they had-- and they had the villagers coming in. Um.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was their reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot of women would cry. Mostly they were old people. There weren't any young-- by that I mean young men. These were 12-, 13-year-old children, boys and girls but mostly girls. Because a lot of the boys were taken away to serve their country, the fatherland too. And oh, elderly people. And some middle-aged guys like this farmer. They were mostly farmers. This British guy had hit with his rifle and killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after you left Bergen-Belsen? What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, went back to Frankfurt again. Was debriefed as usual. Uh, this time they had-- some of the SHAEF officers had brought back some film of-- that the British had taken of Bergen-Belsen, and we reviewed some of the film, which we hadn't seen all of it, you know. We-- we were there as their guests. It was British territory. And even though they-- we were allies and everything, you pretty much had to behave like to their standards. We were fed by them very good, treated very well. Uh, but they wanted you to know that you were on their turf. And I think all the decisions were made by British officers in terms of burning the camp and so on like that. But by the time I left, there was hardly anybody, any-- any people, humans, inmates left in the camp. They had been sent as far away as Switzerland, uh, to recuperate.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the-- about the middle of April. Bergen-Belsen. Actually, we went to see-- I've got these mixed up in terms of sequence. It was-- it was Buchenwald. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau, Bergen-Belsen, and yeah, that's right, and Mauthausen. And Mauthausen-- so we're through with Bergen-Belsen. The British burned it down. I have been back to visit the memorial. It's a very peaceful place with appropriate memorials and identifications. But it was in the Russian zone. So now it's much better than it was under the Russians. Uh, OK. I didn't expect-- what I was hoping for before the war ended on May 8, and I requested and so did Captain Snyder, to be reassigned, because we didn't think we should have to do this anymore. And we were said, OK, well no. We were told by somebody from SHAEF, the Seventh Army headquarters, you guys, you've done-- we'll get some more new guys in here. But you know, the war is almost over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we said, yeah, where have we heard that before? But we want back in, Snyder especially. And he's the guy that got me busted. We both got busted. What we did, real quick like and then you'll know, after the war ended and we were back to our unit, we decided to go to Berlin, to Tempelhof, which was a no-no, Russians. So we flew two airplanes, two P-47 airplanes, went to Tempelhof, and we had a whole cigar box full of watches that we had got everybody's watch. The Russians were nuts about watches, and we had probably a couple hundred watches. I don't know. Whose watches?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the guys, everybody. You know, we're going to sell these watches to the Russians and make a fortune. We had our GI .45s and these two GI air-- we were AWOL. It was a restricted zone. We weren't supposed to be there. Snyder was the guy that I told you, the little cocky guy. He talked me into going. And we landed, and by God they me us in half tracks with machine guns. Scared the peewaddin out of me. But Snyder wasn't scared of anything, and he's arguing with these Russian officers, and they've got these damn machine guns over their shoulders which they're pointing at us. And I'm trying to tell them to shut up. And he's about 5'5, and just a cocky little guy. And he wore his captain-- everything that you could-- every place that you could wear his captain's bars, he did-- your shoulders, your collars, your flight cap. And he had those damn things tattooed on his butt, I suppose. Really, really proud being-- so anyhow, they put us in this half track and they take us to a shack out there away from the main part of the airport, which is a big airport. American airplanes over the damn place, which we had Lend Leased to the Russians. Everything except B-29s they had-- fighters, bombers, medium, light, heavy bombers, everything. C-47s. Millions of dollars worth of airplanes. American trucks, American jeeps. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So they stuck us in this place. They took our guns away, took our wallets away, took our-- I had a charm-- an ID bracelet, sterling silver on, and they took that away. Uh, I had my-- oh, I didn't have my-- I did. I had my dad's ring on. They took that away. It's in the picture of me with that airplane.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Snyder had fly glasses. They took this fly glasses away and I think I had-- I didn't have any-- mine was on my helmet, my flight helmet. And they put us in this damn place, which was like a big box really. It was a room, a crate kind of, with a little window, homemade window. And they left us in there for about an hour. Then here comes some Russian guys, officers, and they we wondering what the hell we were doing. And he got belligerent, and I was trying-- and they spoke English. And these were from the intelligence. I said, we just came up here to sell these watches. And Snyder was getting mad and acting mad because they were holding us. And he thought, you know, we were allies and you guys, we helped you win the war and blah, blah, blah, blah. And this Russian guy-- who outranked him probably by 10 or 15 ranks-- just kind of looked down at him. And he said, well, we want a full tank of fuel. We're leaving. We want to refuel our airplanes and we-- we're leaving. And we're taking all our stuff with us. The guy says, in kind of broken English, but he was sharp. A lot smarter than we were. He said, no, you are leaving with your airplanes. Everything else stays. The watches stay, your weapons stay, you get no fuel. And I immediately told the guy, we can't make it back without refueling. He said, well, you make it as far as you can. And so they let us go, but they kept everything else we had. And we had to land on the Autobahn. We were 200 to 300 miles short of our destination. We decided we're going to have to land or crash, so we landed on the Autobahn. We buzzed it two or three times and landed on the Autobahn. And we got 80 octane gasoline from these truck drivers in their GI cans, which is not very good for that airplane. It's worse than putting high-octane gasoline in a low-octane engine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened in the end, because we need to get back to Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it doesn't burn fast enough. It doesn't give you the power that you need.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we get back to Mauthausen, shortly after we finish this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Well, the bottom line is we got back. We got court-martialed. We got busted and we got fined six months' pay, both of us. Because we had caused an international incident is what we had done. Anyhow, we made it back, but we were busted to enlisted men. They took our commissions away, and we could have gone to prison, but thank God we didn't. I never-- I was one scared kid, I tell you. But anyhow, OK, Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get to Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got to Mauthausen on May 5. And it had just been liberated. We didn't go in until the next day or so. And the General Patton, a tank, I think it was the Fourth Armored Division had liberated Mauthausen. And when we went in, we were in Linz, in a hotel, and we kept hearing rumors about the war winding down and could be over any day and just scuttlebutt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were taken into Mauthausen by officers from General Patton's Third Army, and the inmates, for the most part, had already-- what had happened was the SS had abandoned Mauthausen and the Viennese police had taken over Mauthausen. So when it was liberated, there were very few Germans or Austrians military on premises. And it was the Viennese police department that was in charge. So it was altogether different than any of the other camps. But it turned out to be, as we learned more about Mauthausen, it turned out to be much worse than any of the other camps that we had seen.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a whole line of terrible atrocities were perpetrated in Mauthausen. Mass executions of American POWs, mass executions-- and this is the week before they were liberated-- of Russian POWs. Mass executions of Spaniards who had been in France. After when they had their civil war, whoever lost, I don't know enough about that, had left Spain and gone to France and they had been captured by the Nazis, sent to the camps. They were not Fascists. I don't know what the hell they were, but the ones that lost the war, the civil war in Spain. I don't know. I know that the one that won, that Franco was a Fascist. Hitler had trained, historically-- it isn't important.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how were they executed?

speaker: interviewee
text: But there are thousands, and they were shot and they were gassed. They had a working gas chamber at Mauthausen, which Mr. Wiesenthal and I got into a hell of an argument about. Because he was in Mauthausen. He was an inmate in Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the argument?

speaker: interviewee
text: The argument was-- he made a statement, which has been quoted by the revisionists and deniers that there were no death camps on German soil during World War II. So he did not-- he classified-- a death camp, by his definition, was Auschwitz, Treblinka, Cherno. Those were death camps, and they were all in Poland. But in reality, Auschwitz was not in Poland, it was in Germany. I mean, Germany had annexed that territory. Now, there's a lot of hair splitting going on about these kind of things, so it's not really germane. But there was a working gas chamber at Mauthausen and they-- every kind of torture imaginable had taken place in Mauthausen. Mass starvation. Mass-- One device at Mauthausen that I saw myself, and I couldn't imagine dying like this, was a little closet not big enough for three people, let's say four people max, four adults. Solid walls, four solid walls. Small ceiling. You crawled in through on a brick opening about this far from the floor. You crawled in and four men got in there. No openings. And then they sealed the crawl space where you crawled in there. And it was-- then you stayed in there for 48 hours. You couldn't move, you couldn't turn, you were body to body. Pressed in like in an elevator, only much smaller. And if you survived, when they got you out, when they opened it up through the bottom-- you couldn't even-- if you died, you couldn't even fall down. Then you had-- they left you in there for another 48 hours if you weren't dead. And the idea of that today frightens me just to think of being put in a place like that and left there. And you can't move, you can't raise your arm to scratch your head or nothing. You just stand there until they let you out. And you can't see, you can't hear, and you can't-- a total-- that is the-- that's the cruelty that I mentioned. And The Other things they did at Mauthausen, they called it parachuting. They take these Jews to the-- and they were really-- and they did a lot of medical experimentation at Mauthausen, too. And I've seen evidence of that since the war. I saw it then too, but they had a doctor. They didn't have Mengele, but they had one just like him who had a thing about dwarfs. And they would kill dwarfs, and they liked to do families, a family that maybe some of them might have two or three dwarfs. I'm not talking about midgets. I'm talking about dwarfs who are just-- who are, you know, uh, not properly formed. They have real short arms, whatever. They would kill them, boil them until all the flesh was gone, and then these doctors would try to figure out why-- what it was that made them be dwarfs. And they had skeletons of these dwarfs there in this-- at Mauthausen. Unreal. I mean - you know, It was just unreal. The parachuting was where they take them-- Mauthausen was in a big quarry, and it was designed this way. There were 176 steps, I believe, from the top of the quarry to the bottom, and they were uneven so that you couldn't just walk down steps. One step might be 18 inches, the next one might be 6. And they made these inmates walk down in the quarry, bring back rocks that weighed-- marble rock that weighed as much as they could carry, 60, 70 pounds, back up these steps until they dropped. And when they dropped, they either killed them or shoved them off into the pit. And that was just every day for the entire time, the entire life of Mauthausen. And it was-- uh, it was recognized even among inmates that had been to other camps that that's the last place they wanted to go was Mauthausen if they knew anything about the camp system, which a lot them did. They'd hear it from other people. Like-- like, uh, Mr. Wiesenthal wasn't liberated from Mauthausen, but he was in Mauthausen, and went through some of this stuff.

words.text: I mean - you know, It was

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you see as far as torture?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHING] Torture chambers. They had some of these devices. Even worse, they had the spikes, the spiked collars and the spiked anklets made out of heavy metal, heavy wrought iron. They had, um-- they had a device. I never could figure out what the hell they used this for until a guy explained to me. It was like you had like you're going to step on a scale, except there were no springs there, just a little metal platform. And above this they had a device that they could crank a steel plate down to your head, like you would measure a person's height, except this was a flat piece. And it was a spring in there with a big piece of metal like a spike that was cocked like a spring. And they'd lower that down onto this inmate's head and fire it like a mousetrap. And that spike would go in their brain. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It didn't kill them right away either, unless they hit the right spot. But these are things that I could never understand why and how man, humans, could come up with stuff like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was a condition of the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad. Very bad. There was cannibalism in Mauthausen. Documented cases of cannibalism. More suicides than in any of the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see yourself? What did you look--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't see-- I didn't see any evidence of cannibalism, because when I got there, most-- the second day, this is a day and a half after its liberation. But we were told by-- there were Americans in there and there were British men, soldiers in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: As far as the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw all the experimental places. I went down the steps with guys, Americans and others. And I could-- and I was in good physical condition and I could barely do it, go up and go down and come back up without carrying anything. And these guys had to do this and carry rocks back up. OK. If you lived six weeks in Mauthausen, you were lucky-- or maybe unlucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any people when you came back? Were there any people in Mauthausen, any inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was-- it was just typical. I didn't see any women or kids, just men. All of them were very emaciated, dirty, filthy, unsanitary, skin and bones. Suicides, as I say, even the Americans said that they would be tempted to go over to the edge of the quarry and just go and jump off because you just couldn't take another day of what they knew was going to happen the next day. The Nazis, the SS, would take a Jew they didn't like-- or a person, but most of them were Jews-- to the edge of the quarry and make the guy-- and say, you know, you jump. We want to see you parachute.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're interested in what you saw, your observations.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see any of that. I didn't. I saw the-- I saw the-- there was a-- I saw the pit there. There was 10,000 people buried, and the pit was open when we were there. It's now a park. Filled with bodies. And the body count was 10,000 based on the plaques that are there now. Oh, when we were there, there was just a bunch of dead bodies in the pit, and they weren't covering them up, they were just putting them in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any medical attention given while you were there that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, yeah. That was priority. Priority was medical attention in every case. Here again, General Patton had-- the sickest inmates were moved into Linz immediately. I mean, they had a constant-- 40 miles from Linz, they had a constant stream of ambulances from every military unit that was available went to Mauthausen and hauled sick inmates to Linz and to other towns around there, hospitals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did you see any medical attention given to the inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, on the spot. Yeah. Blood transfusions, plasma, dressings of wounds, setting of broken limbs.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they set up the operating theater, the medical theater? What did that look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't have-- they didn't set it up at Mauthausen, except for emergency cases, and it was in a building. It was a case where, let's get them to Linz, where they can get medical care. They emptied the hospital, the German-- the Austrian hospital in Linz, and they just made them go someplace else, moved them into homes or whatever unless they were dying.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there until the 9th-- the 10th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, about-- let's see. Five? Five days, and the war ended while we were in Mauthausen. Actually, we were in Linz when the war-- when I got the word that the war was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what day was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: May 8, 1945. And we had our radios. We tuned in. We were told about what was going on in New York City, in London, in Paris, and all these big cities, the parties. And we had some booze, and we sat around in our nice hotel room in Linz. And we were so damn depressed that we tried to party, but we couldn't party. It just-- it didn't mean a damn thing. If anything, expression-wise, it was, God damn it, I don't want it to be over yet. You know, we're not through with this. We need to finish this. These are from guys that-- that didn't want to-- they didn't want it to stop like that. They wanted it to stop when they were fighting. After what they'd seen, they wanted revenge. They wanted to avenge. If there's a motive, I don't know whether I'm saying this right, it was, we have to find a way to avenge these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think the camps weren't bombed?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my opinion-- and this is only my opinion. And I never had an opportunity. I could have bombed Mauthausen, I could've bombed Dachau, and I could have-- or strafed them both, but it was-- we were not told not to, but we were told to-- that was not a priority. It wouldn't do any good. Well, first of all, we did know about them. You know, I didn't know that Mauthausen was where Mauthausen was until I went to Mauthausen. I didn't know where Dachau was until I went to Dachau. We knew there were camps, but we weren't looking for that kind of stuff. The guys that discovered the camps weren't looking for the camps. Two camps were liberated on purpose by a general officer saying that is our destination. And it was, uh, Dachau and Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop the tape.
